text: Over a billion people speak Mandarin, but almost none of them call it that. Mandarin is a name foreigners gave it, and the real story of how it took over China begins before Rome was ever an empire. Because long before Rome ruled anything, China had already risen and fallen through dynasties going back more than 3,000 years. But China back then wasn't one country, it was a patchwork of rival states, locked in near constant war, dozens of kingdoms, with different rulers, different armies, and different spoken tongues. Then, in 221 BC, one state finally conquered the rest, forced them into a single empire, and standardized the writing so everyone wrote the same characters. That first empire didn't last long, but the dynasty after it did, the Han. The Han ruled for four centuries and became so foundational that, to this day, most Chinese call themselves Han, and their language Hanyu, the language of Han. But here's the catch, unifying the writing is not the same as unifying the speech. For 2,000 years, Chinese across the empire wrote alike but spoke languages they couldn't understand out loud. So to actually govern, the officials settled on one shared spoken standard, based on the northern capital dialect. They called it Guan Hua, the speech of the officials. And that's where our word is born. When Portuguese traders reached China in the 1500s, they called those robed officials Mandarins. So in Europe, the officials' language became Mandarin, a foreign name for the language of power. Then, last century, that same northern standard was formalized, taught in every school, and pushed through radio and TV until it became the shared voice of 1.4 billion people. And they gave it a new, surprisingly humble name, Putonghua, which means simply, common speech, everyday, ordinary talk. So the exotic, powerful name the West knows, to a billion speakers back home, it's just normal language. Want to actually start learning Chinese?
